topic: FAIL-004 (W020 Roaring 40s 500hPa) EXP-134 rewrite — post-integration review
change_summary: EXP-134 replaced the FAIL-004 paragraph to (a) drop the unjustified AAO σ ↔ 500hPa % equivalence, (b) add an explicit NOAA CPC AAO citation with URL, (c) acknowledge the AAO/700hPa vs 500hPa pressure-level mismatch, and (d) add two compound paragraphs on the Domain 10 threshold inconsistency and the atmospheric-only 3-month rule asymmetry.
our_argument_summary: The dome predicted a ≥3% 500hPa geopotential height anomaly in 40–60°S for March 12–19, 2026 — equivalent to ~165m at climatological ~5500m. The AAO (a 700hPa proxy) averaged only +0.78σ during that week, indicating unremarkable Southern Annular Mode conditions; a 3% 500hPa anomaly is an extreme event (roughly 3–5σ) that clearly did not occur even on this generous proxy. The dome then labeled the outcome 'refined' and introduced a 3-month minimum window rule for atmospheric predictions. The paragraph now adds two compound arguments: (1) Domain 10 of the Live Power Dashboard uses an entirely different threshold for the same phenomenon ('AAO < 0σ while SAA decay >50 nT/yr'), showing post-hoc threshold selection; (2) the 3-month window rule is applied only to atmospheric predictions (which failed) while magnetic predictions with the same test-window length were counted as confirmed — a selectivity that functions as a verdict in disguise.

holes_found.description: The claim 'The retroactive 3-month minimum window rule applies exclusively to atmospheric predictions — which happen to be the predictions that failed. Magnetic predictions with identically short test windows were counted as confirmed without any such requirement' is a strong structural argument but is not backed by a primary-source citation in the paragraph. The raw-text dump in raw-text/*.txt contains no text like 'refined,' 'minimum window,' or '3-month' in connection with prediction methodology (grep confirms: the only '3 month' match in raw-text is Domain 18's falsification criterion 'Wind speed <15 m/s for 3 months', which is a falsification threshold, not a window-length rule). The 3-month rule and its atmospheric-only scope appear to be reconstructed from uncounted-failures.json's dome_label='refined' and our own inference, not from a verifiable dome-authored document. A dome defender can reply: 'Show me where I wrote that atmospheric predictions now require 3-month windows but magnetic ones do not. That's your paraphrase, not my rule.' This is the most vulnerable claim in the paragraph.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) cite a specific dome source document (status_history.json entry, tracking page URL with archive date, or model.html section) where the 3-month rule is stated and scoped to atmospheric predictions, or (b) soften to 'the pattern of enforcement — atmospheric predictions were declared 'refined' for having short windows while magnetic predictions with identically short windows were counted as confirmed — functions as an atmospheric-only rule in practice, whether or not the dome states it formally.' Option (b) is a fair restatement that doesn't require proving the rule was explicitly codified.

holes_found.description: The paragraph rests its 'prediction failed' conclusion on a 700hPa AAO proxy for a 500hPa prediction, while in the same paragraph criticizing the dome for mixing metrics ('500hPa ≥3% above climatology' vs. Domain 10's 'AAO < 0σ while SAA decay >50 nT/yr'). A dome defender can invoke symmetry: 'You criticize us for using incompatible metrics for the same phenomenon, then you judge our 500hPa prediction using a 700hPa index. That's the same error.' The paragraph partially defends itself by saying 'even using this generous proxy' and explicitly acknowledges the pressure-level mismatch, and the quantitative argument (a 3% anomaly ≈ 165m ≈ 3–5σ extreme event would register in any reasonable proxy) is defensible. But it would be stronger with direct ERA5 or MERRA-2 reanalysis data for the 40–60°S band, March 12–19, 2026, and a one-sentence justification for why AAO at +0.78σ strongly rules out a 3% 500hPa anomaly (essentially: the AAO correlates ~0.7–0.8 with SH mid-latitude 500hPa heights, so a 3% anomaly would require AAO well beyond +3σ).
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence stating the AAO–500hPa correlation magnitude (literature value ~0.7–0.8 for SH mid-latitudes) and the implication that +0.78σ AAO rules out a 3% 500hPa extreme. Optional stronger move: cite the ERA5 500hPa anomaly for 40–60°S, March 12–19, 2026 directly (available from Copernicus Climate Data Store) — this eliminates the proxy-mixing objection entirely.

holes_found.description: The 'retroactively labeled the original prediction "refined"' assertion cites no primary source either. Our uncounted-failures.json records dome_label='refined' for FAIL-004 (added 2026-04-09), but the paragraph does not point the reader to where the dome itself uses that word. A dome defender may claim the word was never used or was used differently. This is a minor hole because the substantive point (the prediction failed and was not counted as falsified) is uncontested by anyone, but the specific word 'refined' carries rhetorical weight and should be sourced.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief inline reference: '(dome's own tracking page labels this prediction "refined" rather than falsified — see monitor.py prediction registry or the dome's FAILURE ANALYSIS section as of 2026-04-05)' or cite status_history.json if it's available. If the word 'refined' is not verbatim from the dome, switch to 'the dome did not count this as a falsification, instead treating it as a window-length error.'

holes_found.description: The atmospheric-vs-magnetic window asymmetry argument would be substantially more persuasive with one or two named examples. Right now it says 'Magnetic predictions with identically short test windows were counted as confirmed' but names no specific magnetic prediction that had a 1-week window and was counted as a WIN. WIN-035/036/037 (SAA cell thresholds, weekly monitoring) and WIN-043 (NMP drift) are candidates. Naming even one makes the contrast concrete instead of rhetorical.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a short parenthetical after 'without any such requirement': 'e.g., WIN-035 (African SAA cell ≤21,795 nT, registered 2026-03-06 and confirmed 2026-03-12 on a 6-day window) was counted as confirmed with a shorter window than FAIL-004.' This converts the asymmetry from rhetoric to a documented case.

holes_found.description: The paragraph says 'the Live Power Dashboard Domain 10 tracks the same Southern Hemisphere atmospheric circulation using "AAO < 0σ while SAA decay >50 nT/yr"' but does not clarify that this Domain 10 criterion is a falsification condition, not a confirmation threshold (per raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 101: 'Falsification: "AAO < 0σ while SAA decay >50 nT/yr"'). A defender could argue that a falsification criterion and a confirmation threshold serve different purposes and therefore do not need to match — so using them as evidence of inconsistent thresholds is an apples-to-oranges comparison. The point still holds (the phenomenon is the same and the quantitative metric is incompatible), but the paragraph should acknowledge that the two are different types of criteria.
holes_found.recommendation: Tweak the phrasing to: 'Domain 10's *falsification condition* for the same Southern Hemisphere atmospheric circulation is "AAO < 0σ while SAA decay >50 nT/yr" — a wholly different metric at a different pressure level. Whether a criterion is framed as a success threshold or a falsification trigger, a single phenomenon should admit a single quantitative test; the dome uses two.' This preempts the 'different criteria serve different purposes' objection.

stronger_arguments.description: If available, cite ERA5/MERRA-2 reanalysis 500hPa height anomalies for the 40–60°S latitude band averaged over March 12–19, 2026. This is the exact data the dome's prediction targets. Any well-formed anomaly in that band should be reportable directly; if the number is, say, +0.3% or −0.4%, it would nail the argument without relying on a proxy. The Copernicus Climate Data Store (CDS) provides ERA5 monthly and daily mean geopotential at pressure levels.
sources: Copernicus Climate Data Store: ERA5 daily 500hPa geopotential (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/)
AMS Journal of Climate literature on SAM–mid-latitude height anomaly correlations

stronger_arguments.description: Add an explicit information-theoretic framing: 'A testing framework whose windows are lengthened only after failures produces a pass rate that is 100% by construction and therefore has zero information content per prediction.' This is the same argument in the last sentence of the paragraph but stated in quantitative terms that make the falsification asymmetry unambiguous.
sources: Popper, The Logic of Scientific Discovery — on the asymmetry between verification and falsification

stronger_arguments.description: Quantify how many dome predictions have had their outcomes or test windows altered after registration. If the dome has moved test-window length or threshold for >1 failed prediction but zero successful ones, the asymmetry is a measurable bias. Even a small count (e.g., 3 of 4 failures retroactively reclassified vs. 0 of N successes) is a compact, defensible figure.
sources: data/uncounted-failures.json entries (FAIL-001 through FAIL-004 + silent removal)
our own counting of dome_label='refined' vs. CONFIRMED outcomes with window changes

kernel_of_truth.description: The rewrite is a genuine improvement over the prior text. The sigma-to-percentage conversion error is removed, a direct citation is present for the AAO value, and the paragraph now explicitly flags the AAO/500hPa pressure-level mismatch — preempting the single strongest dome-defender objection. The core argument (the prediction failed, the dome did not count it as falsified, the window-length rule was changed after the failure) is essentially correct on the available evidence. The Domain 10 threshold-inconsistency compound argument is a real finding, verified directly in raw-text/08-live-power.txt:101. The closing sentence ('a methodological rule that is enforced only against the outcomes that were already failures is not a rule; it is a verdict in disguise') is the paragraph's strongest line and is philosophically sound.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The remaining vulnerabilities are about sourcing and phrasing, not about the underlying argument. The dome cannot plausibly claim FAIL-004 was confirmed by a +0.78σ AAO; it cannot plausibly claim a 3% 500hPa anomaly occurred; and it has in fact introduced different thresholds for the same phenomenon. The rewrite wins on substance. What it loses on is verifiability trail — two of the more rhetorically sharp claims ('atmospheric-only rule', 'retroactively labeled refined') depend on inferences from our tracker (uncounted-failures.json) rather than on direct dome-authored text. If a motivated defender demands to see where the dome wrote those words, our paragraph currently has no direct answer. Fixing that sourcing does not require rewriting the argument, only citing where each claim lives in dome source material.

text_fingerprint.verdict: N/A — section prose, not WIN entry

citations_verified: NOAA CPC AAO daily index URL (https://www.cpc.ncep.noaa.gov/products/precip/CWlink/daily_ao_index/aao/aao.shtml) — resolves to the correct CPC AAO product page
Domain 10 threshold 'AAO < 0σ while SAA decay >50 nT/yr' — verified in raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 101
FAIL-004 entry in data/uncounted-failures.json with dome_label='refined' — verified
citations_unchecked: The specific '+0.78σ' AAO value for the week of 2026-03-12 — the URL is provided but the actual value for that week has not been independently pulled from the CPC archive in this review. If the dome's own monitoring or our poller captured this value, the provenance should be documented. (The c2 review's quantitative_verification did not verify this number either.)

The dome's use of the exact word 'refined' for FAIL-004 — our tracker has it as dome_label, but the raw-text dump does not contain any 'refined' occurrences. Worth verifying against the dome's live tracking page or a captured screenshot.

The claim that the 3-month minimum window rule was added to the registry after FAIL-004 and applies only to atmospheric predictions — no primary source in raw-text or monitor.py confirms this. If a dome commit or tracking-page diff is available, it should be cited.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender would make three arguments: (1) 'You are judging our 500hPa prediction using a 700hPa AAO proxy, and in the same paragraph you criticize us for using different metrics for the same phenomenon. Which is it?' (2) 'You accuse us of applying a 3-month window rule only to atmospheric predictions, but you have cited no source showing we ever wrote such a rule. Show me the registry entry or the commit. Otherwise this is your paraphrase of our behavior, not our documented methodology.' (3) 'You claim we labeled FAIL-004 "refined" retroactively, but the raw-text dump your own review reads from contains no occurrence of that word. Where exactly did we write this?' The first two are substantive; the third is narrower but embarrassing if true. A defender might also add: 'Your own quantitative check of my 500hPa prediction depends on pulling the actual CPC AAO archive for week 2026-03-12, which your prior cycle 2 review admitted was unverified. You have not pulled it either.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Address each defense in turn. (1) Add the AAO–500hPa correlation sentence (~0.7–0.8 correlation for SH mid-latitudes) to justify the proxy logic — or, better, cite direct ERA5 reanalysis. (2) Either produce a primary citation for the 3-month rule or soften the language to 'a pattern of enforcement' as per the first moderate hole. (3) Source the word 'refined' to a dated dome tracking-page capture or switch to 'the dome did not count this as a falsification.' None of these fixes changes the argument's substance; all three close the rhetorical gaps a defender would exploit.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: No internal inconsistency with adjacent WINs. WIN-024 (Roaring 40s = SAA boundary, a geographic claim) and the FAIL-004 paragraph (magnitude claim) address different aspects of the same latitude band and do not conflict. WIN-061 (Schumann suppression during G3) parallels FAIL-002's reversed-outcome pattern. WIN-035 (African SAA cell ≤21,795 nT, confirmed in 6 days) is a candidate example of a short-window magnetic prediction counted as a WIN — useful for sharpening the asymmetry argument in the paragraph (see moderate hole #4 above). No cross-WIN contradictions found.

claims_checked: 500hPa climatological height at 50°S ≈ 5500m — correct (standard atmosphere and climatology agree on ~5500–5550m)
A +0.78σ AAO corresponds to unremarkable SAM conditions well within ±1σ — correct
Domain 10 threshold string — verified verbatim in raw-text/08-live-power.txt:101
quantitative_verification.computations: Arithmetic is clean. The more substantive quantitative claim — that +0.78σ AAO rules out a ≥3% 500hPa anomaly in 40–60°S — depends on the AAO–500hPa mid-latitude height correlation. Typical SH SAM/AAO–500hPa geopotential correlations in the 40–60°S band are ~0.7–0.8 (Thompson & Wallace 2000, Marshall 2003). A ≥3% anomaly at 500hPa (~165m) would correspond to well beyond +3σ on the local distribution (climatological daily SD ~30–40m at 50°S), so observation of AAO at +0.78σ is strong circumstantial evidence against. A direct ERA5 pull would close the loop but is not required for the argument to be defensible.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: None of the quantitative statements in the new paragraph are arithmetically wrong. The only unverified numbers are the +0.78σ AAO weekly average (URL provided but value not independently pulled) and the frequency of the '≥3%' threshold in the historical record (not stated in the paragraph but implied to be extreme). Neither gap changes the conclusion.

deeper_analysis:
The EXP-134 rewrite does three valuable things: it removes a quantitative error (the sigma-to-percentage conversion the c2 review flagged as moderate), it adds a verifiable citation (NOAA CPC URL), and it acknowledges an important methodological limitation (the AAO is a 700hPa proxy) instead of hiding it. The decision to add two compound paragraphs — on Domain 10 threshold inconsistency and on atmospheric-only 3-month window asymmetry — substantially sharpens the argument beyond the c2 text. The Domain 10 point is particularly strong because it is directly verifiable in raw-text and turns an inconsistency into an own-goal for the dome.

The remaining weaknesses are sourcing weaknesses, not argument weaknesses. Two rhetorically sharp claims ('atmospheric-only rule', 'retroactively labeled refined') rest on inferences from our tracker rather than on citable dome source material. This does not make them wrong — they are likely correct — but it exposes them to a 'show me where I wrote that' rebuttal that currently has no direct reply. The fix is straightforward: cite primary sources where available, and soften language to 'pattern of enforcement' where no codified rule exists.

The biggest conceptual tension is that this paragraph criticizes the dome for using incompatible metrics for the same phenomenon (Domain 10 vs. W020) while itself using AAO (700hPa) to judge a 500hPa prediction. The paragraph partially addresses this with 'even using this generous proxy' and the pressure-level footnote, but a defender can still claim the paragraph commits the same error it criticizes. The cleanest fix is a direct ERA5 pull; the cheaper fix is a one-sentence justification of the AAO–500hPa correlation.

Verdict: current text holds (the prediction did fail, the dome did treat it as non-falsification, the methodological rule was asymmetric in practice). Recommended action is minor_edit — sourcing improvements, not rewrites. Confidence 0.80, down slightly from cycle 2's 0.82 because cycle 2 did not weight the 'sourcing trail for the 3-month rule' objection as heavily as it deserves. The argument wins; the footnotes need tightening.
